---
subtitle: "PHIL101B -- Mind"
title: "<font style='font-size:1em;'>🗓️ Chapter 15<br/> Consciousness</font>"
author: Prof. [Reynolds](#)
institute: '[Stetson University](#)'
date: 02 September 2026
date-meta: 14 March 2023
date-format: "DD MMM YYYY"
toc: false
toc-depth: 1
toc-title: "Consciousness"
center-title-slide: false
from: markdown+emoji
format:
  revealjs:
    theme:
      league
    data-background-image: city.jpg
    transition:
      zoom
    fig-responsive: true
    slide-number: true
    showNotes: 'separate-page'
    incremental: true
    mouse-wheel: false
    auto-play-media: true
    preview-links: auto
    logo: /figures/icons/course_favicon.png
    css: /css/styles_slides.css
    footer: 'PHIL101B -- Mind'
---

## {background-image=alone.jpg}

> Today's experience, which is not tomorrow's, has in it some hints and implications which are tomorrow on the horizon of today. Each man's experience may be added to by the experience of other men, who are living in his day or have lived before; and so a common world of experience, larger than that of his own observations, can be lived in by each man. [@lang07]

::: {.notes}

Lets imagine a complete broad historical analysis of the development of knowledge in the West.

This development first begins with a number of assumptions, for instance that my inner life shares a relationship to the physical world.

That my experiences can be explained by my observations.

:::

# The Zombie {background-image=./dawn.jpeg}

* [ ] Some Background
* [ ] Initial Criteria for Consciousness
* [ ] Is Data a Zombie?

::: {.notes}

Is Data conscious?

* Creation & Background: Discovered by Starfleet on Omicron Theta in 2338 as the sole survivor of the Crystalline Entity attack, Data was built by Dr. Noonien Soong, who previously created an unstable android named Lore.
* Starfleet Career: He joined the academy in 2341, graduated in 2345, and later served as the second officer and chief operations officer on the USS Enterprise-D and USS Enterprise-E.
* Abilities: Data has immense memory capacity, high computational speeds, and, according to a user on The Robot Hall of Fame, super-strength. He is immune to most biological diseases.
* Personality & Evolution: Though designed with superior cognitive abilities, he initially lacked emotions, which he often sought to understand. Later in the series, he acquires an "emotion chip". He is known to be a "humanity's outsider" character, often questioning his own humanity.
* Personal Life: He owned a cat named Spot, pursued painting, music, and poetry, and was close friends with Chief Engineer Geordi La Forge.
* Enduring Legacy: Data appears across The Next Generation television series, four feature films, and the series Picard.

:::

---

### Ai Sim Generation

[ai town](https://convex.dev/ai-town)

[life sim](https://www.thelifesim.com/)

::: {.notes}

We did the second one yesterday but I would not implement it in class again I think. It seems to me that one question we should be asking with regard to ai agents, is whether or not they are conscious. Even apart from this question however, there is a related question about our obligations towards them. It seems that the way we treat games like GTA etc, is a predictor of how we treat one another, and how we will treat sims in the future. Further, the only way to mitigate this aggression, is if the other entity represents some potential harmful response. If the sim can build a nuclear weapon before they are destroyed, then they will be treated with respect and autonomy.

Life sim allows the user to define a life by selecting attributes they think is appropriate for that kind of life. If a person is divorced and lives in Gilbert AZ, then of course they live in a trailer and have many cats. Of course they post regularly on Reddit.

We see a similar question arising with Data. Ultimately, this problem will become *The Problem with other minds*.

:::

## Developments in the Philosophy of Mind {background-image=landscape.jpg}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}

:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
1. Avoid causal mysteries about the working of the mind.
2. Provide a reasonable account of what we can know or infer about the mental states of other people.

:::

:::

::: {.notes}

I am meant to assume that my thoughts are free and uniquely my own and that they are not limited in any way.

* I have visual, auditory, thought, experiences

:::

## {background-image=planet-home.png}

::: {.r-fit-text}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="25%"}

:::

::: {.column width="75%"}

> Human beings are complex biological organisms and as such are part of the natural order, being subject to the same laws of nature as everything else in the world. [@bott03]

:::

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

* Why is there consciousness in the universe?
* How do physical causes give rise to immaterial experiences
* How is there subjective experience in an objective world?

* A philosophy of mind: Our minds relationship to the outside world.
* A philosophy of psychology: The outside world's relationship to our minds.

:::

:::

:::

:::

::: {.notes}

However, according to naturalism:

If there were limits to what I can think about, or if my thoughts in themselves are predetermined in anyway, it will only be because there are some "fundamental assumptions": these would have a direct causal relationship to prior events.

My experiences represent a kind of training data that includes perceptions, reactions, feelings, and ideas. In other words, my thoughts are the outputs of a complex process of input, (my experiences), and the functional or algorithmic activity of my inner thought life.

The dichotomy and attempts to answer it can be summed up nicely.

:::

## Attempts in the philosophy of mind

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="65%"}

> the dichotomy of all reality into *inner experience and outer world*, subject and object, private reality and public truth. [@lang07, 12]

:::

::: {.column width="35%"}


:::

:::

::: {.notes}

When we think about these assumptions, we are likely also thinking about that specific behavior that we are performing, thought, and those inputs whatever they are.

However, upon reflection, it would seem that the quality of thought, even more specifically, what is happening such that my experience of the phenomenon of "thinking", seems so drastically different from any other experiences that I have.

That my thoughts are in an important way, qualitatively different from all of my other experiences that are not thought.

:::

## The New Epoch {background-image=ruins.jpg}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

* Dualism
* Logical Behaviorism
* Identity Theory
* Functonalism
* Theory-Theory

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

* Folk-Psychology
* Methodological Behaviorism
* Cognition and the Functional Paradigm
* Cognition and Computation
* Connectionism and Neural Networks

:::

:::

::: {.notes}

We are already familiar with dualism. Logical behaviorism, posited by Gilbert Ryle (1949) is that it is a mistake to think of the "mental" as a set of "inner causes of behavior", but rather as a set of dispositions to behave and patters of behavior. We might also think that mental states are identical to states or events of the brain. Functionalists classify a thing by what it does. Mental states then are just states with a particular role, whatever they are and theory-theorists argue that mental state concepts come from their position in the causal structure and functioning of the mind.

:::

## {background-image=desolate-landscape.jpg}

### Dualism

::: {.r-fit-text}

* Strong Dualism: Mind and Body are distinct
  * mind and body are distinct,
  * bodies are physical things that are extended in space,
  * they are subject to the laws of physics and chemistry,
  * minds to not take up any space.
* Weak Dualism: Mental properties are not physical
  * mind and body properties may share a single object like a person
  * but mental properties are not physical
  * can vary independently
  * minds to not take up any space.
:::

::: {.notes}

These three chapters look at different theories about the mind. For our purposes, we will primarily be looking at two philosophical disciplines which ask questions about the mind.

If you are listening, you likely know a lot about the mind already. As such, you will likely already be familiar with a wide range of mental phenomena such as thoughts, perceptions, emotions, moods, and bodily sensations.

According to ***Strong dualism***

* mind and body are distinct,
* bodies are physical things that are extended in space,
* they are subject to the laws of physics and chemistry,
* minds to not take up any space.

 According to ***Weak dualism***

* mind and body properties may share a single object like a person
* but mental properties are not physical
* can vary independently
* minds to not take up any space.

:::

## Logical Behaviorism $\Rightarrow$ Physicalism

Forms of physicalism

* Supervenience
* Emergence
* Intelligibility

::: {.notes}

What the above says is that if logical behaviorism is true, then physicalism is true.

Recall that according to @bott03, logical behaviorism is the view that an observation of our internal experiences:

* feelings, emotions, beliefs, and thoughts,
* should not lead to a discussion about whether these experiences, the causes of the mental states, are grounded in physical reality or not.
* Rather it is a talk about a disposition to behavior.

However, logical behaviorism was only offered as an account of *conceptual analysis*.

What logical behaviorism says is that "mental events or states should actually be construed as 'iffy' or conditional statements about people's actual and possible behavior".

Logical behaviorism is an important part of understanding our experiences and more importantly, the divergent nature of our subjective and objective experiences. It gives us a way to account for the difference between our experiences and observations where we experience the world subjectively, but our observations do not seem to support those experiences.

A challenge in building a physicalist theory, is developing theories corresponding with the physical.

* What does it mean to be physical?
  * Chalmers gives an account of the physical that includes small constituent components of matter and accompanying facts about how those constituent components of matter are arranged.
  * What ever theory we come up with, needs to explain the difference between our experiences and our observations however.

:::

# References
